text: Some excellent points by Wittens on why VR is failing to catch on at present. I agree that VR will take a very long time get right, and the Pokemon Go success indicates to me that AR will outsell VR.

text: I looked at the Go language code on GitHub. I couldn't see anywhere it draws to the screen. So is the visual editor not part of that code base, and is something else you have written, which is not available? i was wondering if you were using Electron or some other framework for the drawing. Yes the JS code does have maltese in it. in one of the date functions. I was looking at one of the main .JS files. When you copy and paste code and include other libraries you do end up with surprising stuff. It doesn't matter, but i can assure you i am not making up words like Xahar and Għada

text:
Personally I like this more than the previous one with the illustrated character. But I think there are still some weaknesses in the delivery compared to the product.

What interests me most about Storybook is that you can create code that uses many different services without having to write a custom integration or find a suitable API client library(which may or may not be an official one). I feel when writing such code, the integration-specific parts tend to dominate the complexity and a lot of effort is duplicated across projects. Being able to outsource all that to a provider dedicated to keeping the integrations working and high-quality seems like a great convenience that people would pay money for.

I feel like this aspect isn't really conveyed well to someone unfamiliar with the product right now.

Also compare to the landing pages of Zapier or IFTT, which much more concisely(and visually) explain the value that the product provides, rather than mockups or screenshots of what the product looks like(which I don't think are very helpful for a landing page, though in storybook's case having some code snippets might be nice):

<https://ifttt.com/>
<https://zapier.com/>

To me Zapier's:
> Easy automation for busy people. Zapier moves info between your web apps automatically, so you can focus on your most important work.
feels more evocative and concrete than:
> Storyscript is a language, editor, database, infra and community all-in-one. Create holistic apps, tools and workflows blazing fast that power your team
which while more descriptive is also more plain and only highlights one rather vague benefit("blazing fast". It also isn't clear here to me _what_ is blazing fast). Compare to Zapier highlighting two concrete ones("easy", "focus on your most important work").

In any case I think the product is exciting and I believe it has a lot of potential, so good luck!

text: Wow there's a lot of stuff to respond to here, but here's a summary of what I'm thinking. My hypothesis is that the barrier to entry for generative art / creative coding / reactive animations is very high because the medium is naturally difficult to map onto imperative code. FRP (or more specifically DCTP) was originally invented to solve this exact problem but hasn't taken off for many reasons: meta-level thinking required for higher order streams, lack of interfaces (text or otherwise) that allow one to manipulate streams in an easily-digestible way, and performance to name a few challenges. None of these seem insurmountable and I think that a tool like this has the potential to blur the line between programmer and artist like Processing intended nearly two decades ago

text: Tools like Core Animation and Greensock are the exact opposite of what I'm looking for. I'm thinking about animation as a pure function from user interaction to pixels on a screen—inherently interrupt-able. frameworks like these seem like solutions created for interfaces where the language didn't have the expressive power to model continuous time

text:
<@ULM3U6275> Examples of good structure editors and/or systems that "work well for both mouse navigation and keyboard navigation"? I actually don't know many! I've seen a few apps that I've personally found convoluted, though.
Notion is a pretty damn good structure editor, though the structure being edited isn't that complex. TreeSheets (<http://strlen.com/treesheets/>) extends the spreadsheet paradigm, but I find some of its controls awkward.

text: Hi there! I'm Tuomas, and the author of <http://Operon.io|Operon.io> -language. I have played with different programming languages for 30+ years, and felt that the existing languages don't work as I would like them to work, on the real life problems related on my field of integration. I created the initial website for the project, as a starting point for the release process (<http://operon.io|http://operon.io>). I haven't yet released the binaries and most of the documentation is yet to be written :grin: I joined here as I felt the purpose of this community is aligned with my mission. I hope to share my thoughts with you, reach some of you to both give and receive feedback. After the project is mature enough I will start to reach out for larger audience. Cheers! :smile:

I have played with different programming languages for 30+ years, and felt that the existing languages don't work as I would like them to work, on the real life problems related on my field of integration. ). I haven't yet released the binaries and most of the documentation is yet to be written

I joined here as I felt the purpose of this community is aligned with my mission. I hope to share my thoughts with you, reach some of you to both give and receive feedback. After the project is mature enough I will start to reach out for larger audience. Cheers!

text: <@UJKKBAMLL> A large contingent of people here are interested in the issues with code, and see the failings of our tools as a consequence of the failings of code itself. If you have things to say about the issues inherent to code, you should share them in a way that we can all read and respond to and reflect on, rather than spin these discussions off to DMs. If you don't want to derail this latest thread of feedback on your homepage that makes perfect sense — I invite you to spin up a new thread over in <#C5T9GPWFL|general> with your thoughts on code itself. That's what this community is for.

text:
My main goals for Curv are:
• Artistic expressiveness. You can make any kind of 2D or 3D art, using a wide variety of techniques.
• Ease of use. This covers a lot of ground, including the IDE and the language itself.
Mathematical purity is not a goal, but there are lots of areas in the design where mathematical purity makes important contributions to artistic expressiveness, ease of use, and ease of engineering. So I use mathematical purity as a tool, not as an end in itself:
• Pure functional programming. This contributes greatly to ease of use, and simplifies the engineering (generating data-parallel rendering code). The semantics of the language are much simpler. A pure functional API for constructing shapes by composing functions is *so much nicer* than a 1990's style API where graphical primitives paint directly onto a canvas and you have to manage a set of global variables containing the current colour, the current line width, the current transformation matrix, etc.
• Abstract algebra, what Haskell calls "equational reasoning". Unlike most programming languages, the equality operator `a==b` is an equivalence relation: `a==a`; `a==b` implies `b==a`; `a==b` and `b==c` implies `a==c`. Unlike most dynamic languages, the boolean operations obey all the axioms of a boolean algebra. For example, unlike in Python Javascript Lisp, the boolean and operator is commutative. This all falls under ease of use: simple language semantics, no surprises.
However, there are some kinds of mathematical purity in Haskell culture that are detrimental to my goals.
• Lazy function calls are a net loss; Curv uses strict argument evaluation in function calls.
• Static typing is a big barrier to learning a language, and is incompatible with live programming, so Curv is dynamically typed.
• I haven't built a Turing complete reactive API yet, but DCTP looks really hard to understand. The Elm language transitioned from "mathematically pure" FRP to "the Elm architecture", which is much easier to understand, and it is still "pure functional" and "reactive". So I'll probably take my lead from Elm, begin with the Elm architecture, and iterate from there.

elements.text: My main goals for Curv are:
Artistic expressiveness. You can make any kind of 2D or 3D art, using a wide variety of techniques.
Ease of use. This covers a lot of ground, including the IDE and the language itself.

Mathematical purity is not a goal, but there are lots of areas in the design where mathematical purity makes important contributions to artistic expressiveness, ease of use, and ease of engineering. So I use mathematical purity as a tool, not as an end in itself:

Pure functional programming. This contributes greatly to ease of use, and simplifies the engineering (generating data-parallel rendering code). The semantics of the language are much simpler. A pure functional API for constructing shapes by composing functions is
so much nicer

than a 1990's style API where graphical primitives paint directly onto a canvas and you have to manage a set of global variables containing the current colour, the current line width, the current transformation matrix, etc.
Abstract algebra, what Haskell calls "equational reasoning". Unlike most programming languages, the equality operator
a==b
is an equivalence relation:
a==a
a==b
implies
b==a
a==b
and
b==c
implies
a==c

. Unlike most dynamic languages, the boolean operations obey all the axioms of a boolean algebra. For example, unlike in Python Javascript Lisp, the boolean and operator is commutative. This all falls under ease of use: simple language semantics, no surprises.
However, there are some kinds of mathematical purity in Haskell culture that are detrimental to my goals.
Lazy function calls are a net loss; Curv uses strict argument evaluation in function calls.
Static typing is a big barrier to learning a language, and is incompatible with live programming, so Curv is dynamically typed.

I haven't built a Turing complete reactive API yet, but DCTP looks really hard to understand. The Elm language transitioned from "mathematically pure" FRP to "the Elm architecture", which is much easier to understand, and it is still "pure functional" and "reactive". So I'll probably take my lead from Elm, begin with the Elm architecture, and iterate from there.

text:
My main goals for Curv are:
• Artistic expressiveness. You can make any kind of 2D or 3D art, using a wide variety of techniques.
• Ease of use. This covers a lot of ground, including the IDE and the language itself.
Mathematical purity is not a goal, but there are lots of areas in the design where mathematical purity makes important contributions to artistic expressiveness, ease of use, and ease of engineering. So I use mathematical purity as a tool, not as an end in itself:
• Pure functional programming. This contributes greatly to ease of use, and simplifies the engineering (generating data-parallel rendering code). The semantics of the language are much simpler. A pure functional API for constructing shapes by composing functions is *so much nicer* than a 1990's style API where graphical primitives paint directly onto a canvas and you have to manage a set of global variables containing the current colour, the current line width, the current transformation matrix, etc.
• Abstract algebra, what Haskell calls "equational reasoning". Unlike most programming languages, the equality operator `a==b` is an equivalence relation: `a==a`; `a==b` implies `b==a`; `a==b` and `b==c` implies `a==c`. Unlike most dynamic languages, the boolean operations obey all the axioms of a boolean algebra. For example, unlike in Python Javascript Lisp, the boolean and operator is commutative. This all falls under ease of use: simple language semantics, no surprises.
However, there are some kinds of mathematical purity in Haskell culture that are detrimental to my goals.
• Lazy function calls are a net loss; Curv uses strict argument evaluation in function calls.
• Static typing is a big barrier to learning a language, and is incompatible with live programming, so Curv is dynamically typed.
• I haven't built a Turing complete reactive API yet, but DCTP looks really hard to understand. The Elm language transitioned from "mathematically pure" FRP to "the Elm architecture", which is much easier to understand, and it is still "pure functional" and "reactive". So I'll probably take my lead from Elm, begin with the Elm architecture, and iterate from there.

Lazy function calls are a net loss; Curv uses strict argument evaluation in function calls.
Static typing is a big barrier to learning a language, and is incompatible with live programming, so Curv is dynamically typed.

I haven't built a Turing complete reactive API yet, but DCTP looks really hard to understand. The Elm language transitioned from "mathematically pure" FRP to "the Elm architecture", which is much easier to understand, and it is still "pure functional" and "reactive". So I'll probably take my lead from Elm, begin with the Elm architecture, and iterate from there.

text: On the subject of AR/VR, what are some good examples of a new technology where lots of well-funded companies make incremental progress on it before it finally becomes "good enough" and goes on to be a hit product? E.g., some counter examples: The iPhone was developed by a team in secrecy at Apple and was released and became successful right away. And the early personal computers, like the Apple II, were based on the work of hobbyists. Can anyone think of a good example of a technology that was in the same position of funding and hype that AR/VR are in today that then became a successful product?

elements.text: On the subject of AR/VR, what are some good examples of a new technology where lots of well-funded companies make incremental progress on it before it finally becomes "good enough" and goes on to be a hit product? And the early personal computers, like the Apple II, were based on the work of hobbyists. Can anyone think of a good example of a technology that was in the same position of funding and hype that AR/VR are in today that then became a successful product?

text: interesting question, it is certainly unusual in how we've all decided that eventually it will be awesome ahead of time

elements.text: interesting question, it is certainly unusual in how we've all decided that eventually it will be awesome ahead of time

text:
<@UE0ETTCG7> Are you looking for products or for technologies? It’s a little unclear because you ask for one and give examples for the other. There were other smartphones before the iPhone, as there were other personal computers before the Apple II. 

Likely, it'll take a few more products in the AR and VR space until one becomes successful, whatever the metric for that is. And I doubt we will be able to tell until a few years passed. It's easy to say that now in hindsight, but I don’t think many people considered the iPhone a success back in 2007.

text: IOT comes to mind.. also a field that sees a LOT of activity and frankly I don't know of anything in the space that is super compelling

text: Does electric cars qualify? Or is it still too early to tell?

elements.text: Does electric cars qualify? Or is it still too early to tell?

text: <@U5STGTB3J> Good question re technology vs. a product. I think "technology" is probably the wrong word. What do you call a particular arrangement of components into a product? Maybe I just mean "product category", e.g., like smart phone or PC. And so with AR/VR we have the product category, but no hit product. Whereas most successful examples I can think of the product category and the hit product seem to come at once?

text: Electric car is a really interesting example. I'm honestly not sure where it sits in all this, but one of the things that's striking about that product is it took an auto industry outsider (Musk & Tesla) to force the industry forward.

elements.text: Electric car is a really interesting example. I'm honestly not sure where it sits in all this, but one of the things that's striking about that product is it took an auto industry outsider (Musk & Tesla) to force the industry forward.

text: I can't tell if the electric car is an example or a counter example. It's such a weird situation, if you haven't seen "Who Killed the Electric Car?" documentary, I recommend it. In it they interviewed a bunch of early electric car customers raving about how much they loved it (the GM EV1), but GM wouldn't let them buy it, only lease it. They had been required by a California law to build it. Instead they got the law repealed, collected all the cars from the customers that were leasing them and destroyed them. I've never seen anything like it.

I've never seen anything like it.

text:
<@UE0ETTCG7> I’m not sure I follow and think your counter examples are actually examples: When Blackberrys were popular, weren’t they smartphones? They came before the iPhone. And there were others before them. Altair came before Apple I.

This reminds me of this distinction between being first, being best, and being cheapest. If I understand you correctly, you’re asking for who was first and best at the same time?

elements.text: I’m not sure I follow and think your counter examples are actually examples: When Blackberrys were popular, weren’t they smartphones? They came before the iPhone. And there were others before them. Altair came before Apple I.
This reminds me of this distinction between being first, being best, and being cheapest. If I understand you correctly, you’re asking for who was first and best at the same time?

text: The core distinction for the iPhone was the touch screen, e.g., if it were an example of an AR/VR like situation, would mean there would be a bunch of companies all really excited about touch screens, and then boom the iPhone, successful product. But it didn't happen that way. I don't remember people talking much about touch screen phones before the iPhone. Or even touch screens in general, though they certainly existed.

elements.text: The core distinction for the iPhone was the touch screen, e.g., if it were an example of an AR/VR like situation, would mean there would be a bunch of companies all really excited about touch screens, and then boom the iPhone, successful product. But it didn't happen that way. I don't remember people talking much about touch screen phones before the iPhone. Or even touch screens in general, though they certainly existed.

text: And my understanding is that the Altair was brought to market by a tiny company. E.g., creating the PC didn't take say, the resources of IBM make it a successful product, like AR/VR appears to need today.

elements.text: And my understanding is that the Altair was brought to market by a tiny company. E.g., creating the PC didn't take say, the resources of IBM make it a successful product, like AR/VR appears to need today.

text: Altair and first Oculus products (before Facebook bought them) look a lot alike, but the successors to the initial Altair were big successes relatively quickly, whereas Oculus's successors haven't gone much of anywhere.

elements.text: Altair and first Oculus products (before Facebook bought them) look a lot alike, but the successors to the initial Altair were big successes relatively quickly, whereas Oculus's successors haven't gone much of anywhere.

text: I guess the core point is that successful products seem to feel more like they’re discovered than invented, e.g., someone discovers that a particular combination of existing technology would make a great product (the iPhone and PC seem to fit this mold). Whereas AR/VR seem to be trying to be willed into existence. I guess this points to me thinking “the next big thing” will be discovered rather than invented, but I’m certainly not taking any bets on this :grin:. Also I’m not even sure that there is a precedent for so many companies investing so much money in products that bring in such a small ROI? Maybe with enough money and resources you can will successful products into existence?

. Also I’m not even sure that there is a precedent for so many companies investing so much money in products that bring in such a small ROI? Maybe with enough money and resources you can will successful products into existence?

text: How about computers? There was tremendous hype about them, starting in the late 1940's. They were called "electronic brains" in the popular press. There was a misconception that they could think, reinforced by pulp science fiction. The first commercial general purpose electronic computer sold to a customer was the Ferranti Mark I in 1951. They sold 2 of them, and lost money. Remington Rand was much more successful with the Univac: they sold 46 of them (starting in 1951). There were lots of companies competing in this space at the time, but only IBM survived. IBM entered the market in 1953 with the 701 (they sold 19 over a 3 year period).

elements.text: How about computers? There was tremendous hype about them, starting in the late 1940's. They were called "electronic brains" in the popular press. There was a misconception that they could think, reinforced by pulp science fiction. The first commercial general purpose electronic computer sold to a customer was the Ferranti Mark I in 1951. They sold 2 of them, and lost money. Remington Rand was much more successful with the Univac: they sold 46 of them (starting in 1951). There were lots of companies competing in this space at the time, but only IBM survived. IBM entered the market in 1953 with the 701 (they sold 19 over a 3 year period).

text: Other examples (massive hype, lots of money => incremental progress, leading eventually to a hit product): automobiles, passenger aircraft.

elements.text: Other examples (massive hype, lots of money => incremental progress, leading eventually to a hit product): automobiles, passenger aircraft.

text: The blackberry wasn't the only smartphone predecessor to the iphone. General Magic designed what I think was the earliest smartphone in 1990. They had massive funding, and were partners with Motorola, AT&T, Sony, Apple, and others, but failed to turn their design into a successful product. Apple stole their IP and released the Newton PDA in 1993, which helped kill them off. In 1994, Motorola released the Envoy Personal Wireless Communicator, and Sony released the Magic Link (using the AT&T network), but both products failed. Neither product had internet access, but relied on dialup data network access for digital communication. The Envoy measured 7.25 by 5.75 by 1.2 inches and weighed 1.6 pounds. The idea was just ahead of its time.

text: I and others would be curious to know what toolchain you used to make your editor. Building complex graphical interactive software like your editor is the goal of several of the FoC projects here, and i am very interested in people's experiences, good and bad, using existing tools like Electron, etc. The Editor is often more work than the product underneath it.
